title: Assassination of Julius Caesar
summary: On the Ides of March 44 BCE, at a meeting of the Roman Senate held in the Curia of Pompey behind the Theatre of Pompey on the Campus Martius, some sixty senators calling themselves the Liberators stabbed the dictator Gaius Julius Caesar twenty-three times at the foot of the statue of his rival. The conspiracy, led by Marcus Junius Brutus and Gaius Cassius Longinus, was meant to save the Roman Republic. Within a generation it had helped destroy it, and the constitutional monarchy of Augustus and his heirs took the Republic's place.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Julius Caesar was fifty-five years old in the spring of 44 BCE and at the height of his power. Born in July 100 BCE into an old but modestly wealthy patrician family, he had climbed the Roman ladder of office as pontifex maximus in 63 BCE, praetor in 62 BCE, and, together with Pompey the Great and Marcus Licinius Crassus, formed the informal alliance later called the First Triumvirate that dominated Roman politics from 60 BCE. His consulship of 59 BCE was followed by nine years of continuous campaigning in Gaul, described in the seven surviving books of his own Commentarii de bello Gallico. By 50 BCE he had extended Roman rule to the Rhine and the Channel, twice crossed the Rhine, and twice invaded Britain.

The alliance disintegrated. Crassus was killed at Carrhae in 53 BCE, and by 49 BCE Pompey and the Senate demanded Caesar surrender his provinces and armies before returning to Rome for a new consulship. On the night of 10 or 11 January 49 BCE Caesar crossed the Rubicon, the stream marking the northern boundary of Italy proper, into a civil war he won in less than five years. Pompey was defeated at Pharsalus in Thessaly in August 48 BCE and murdered on landing in Egypt weeks later; his Republican partisans were beaten at Thapsus in North Africa in 46 BCE and at Munda in Spain in March 45 BCE.

Caesar returned to Rome in the autumn of 45 BCE the undisputed master of the Roman world. The Senate voted him a bewildering series of honours: dictator for life in February 44 BCE (dictator perpetuo), the title of parens patriae (father of the fatherland), the right to wear the triumphator's purple robe and laurel wreath at all times, the placing of his statue among those of the seven kings on the Capitol, the renaming of the month Quintilis as Julius. His head appeared on Roman coins struck in his lifetime, an unprecedented honour for a living Roman.

He used his power to legislate at pace: a debt-relief programme, extension of citizenship to the Cisalpine Gauls, land settlements for eighty thousand veterans in colonies from Spain to Africa, a new solar calendar of 365 and a quarter days (the Julian calendar, in force in Rome from 1 January 45 BCE and in Christian Europe until 1582 CE), and plans for a campaign against Parthia to avenge Crassus and a canal at the Isthmus of Corinth.

Opposition took shape in the Senate. Some conspirators were former Pompeians whom Caesar had pardoned, notably Marcus Junius Brutus, praetor for 44 BCE, whose ancestor Lucius Junius Brutus had expelled the last king of Rome in 509 BCE and whose mother Servilia had been Caesar's longtime lover. Others were disappointed Caesarians, above all Gaius Cassius Longinus, praetor and brother-in-law of Brutus, and Decimus Junius Brutus Albinus, a senior Caesarian officer whom Caesar had named as his heir in the second degree of his will. Cicero, though not in the plot, had written openly against Caesar's tyranny. A series of incidents in the winter of 45 to 44 BCE, above all the offering of a royal diadem to Caesar at the festival of the Lupercalia on 15 February by his co-consul Mark Antony, which Caesar publicly refused three times, alarmed even those Romans who might have accepted a dictatorship in fact if not in name.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Senate meeting of the Ides of March, 15 March 44 BCE, was fixed as the day of action. It would be the last regular meeting before Caesar departed for the Parthian campaign on 18 March; his absence would then last for years. The venue was the Curia of Pompey, a great rectangular hall attached to the massive Theatre of Pompey complex on the Campus Martius outside the pomerium (the sacred boundary of the city); the Curia Hostilia in the Forum had burned down in 52 BCE and its replacement was still under construction. About sixty senators were involved in the plot, according to the count of Nicolaus of Damascus, though only some twenty are named in later sources.

Ancient tradition insists that Caesar received warnings. The soothsayer Spurinna had told him to beware the Ides of March; his wife Calpurnia had dreamed of holding his bloodied body; the entrails of a sacrifice that morning were said to be inauspicious. Caesar was ill in the days before, possibly with what modern historians suspect was mild epilepsy or a viral illness, and had considered postponing the meeting. Decimus Brutus, one of the plotters, arrived at his house and talked him into attending; not to appear would look weak and postpone the whole month's business.

Caesar was carried in a litter through the streets, still discussing his forthcoming journey with the astrologer Spurinna as he passed the Theatre. In the vestibule of the Curia, a Greek teacher named Artemidorus of Cnidos pressed a note of warning into his hand, but Caesar did not read it. Inside, the senators rose as usual when he entered. He took his gilded chair between the columns, in front of a statue of the dead Pompey, and began to hear petitions.

The conspirators had allotted roles. Tillius Cimber approached first, ostensibly to beg mercy for his exiled brother, and grasped the fold of Caesar's toga at both shoulders. Caesar tried to shake him off. Publius Servilius Casca, standing behind him, then drew a dagger from under his toga and struck at Caesar's neck. The blow was clumsy, cutting only the shoulder. Caesar reportedly cried out in Latin, Casca, you villain, what are you doing? and seized Casca's arm. But the other conspirators, drawing daggers concealed in scroll-cases and folds of clothing, closed around him. Caesar attempted to defend himself, wrapping his toga about his head to fall with dignity. He was struck twenty-three times, according to the post-mortem performed by the physician Antistius. Only one wound, the second, driven into his ribs, was judged mortal in itself; the rest were superficial, and death was by loss of blood. The most famous of the alleged last words, in Greek, kai su teknon (You too, my child), addressed to Marcus Brutus, is reported by Suetonius as a rumour and is missing from the earlier account of Nicolaus of Damascus; Shakespeare's Latin Et tu, Brute is early modern. Caesar fell at the base of Pompey's statue and, tradition records, the marble was spattered with his blood.

The assassins had rehearsed their next move poorly. Brutus stepped into the middle of the hall, holding up his bloodied dagger, and called on Cicero by name to congratulate them on the restoration of the Republic. But the other senators, terrified, fled through the doors and out into the Campus Martius. The city outside panicked. The Liberators withdrew, still armed, to the Capitoline Hill and waited for the crowds to declare for them. They never did. Caesar's body lay in the Curia for hours until three of his slaves carried it home on a litter, one arm hanging over the side.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The political improvisation of the next weeks decided everything that followed. Mark Antony, Caesar's co-consul, had been detained outside the Curia at the critical moment by Trebonius and so escaped harm. On 17 March he convened the Senate in the Temple of Tellus and negotiated a compromise: the assassins would not be prosecuted, but Caesar's acta (his official measures and appointments) would stand and he would receive a public funeral. In practice this handed the political initiative to Antony, who held Caesar's papers, and to the young Gaius Octavius, Caesar's great-nephew and, as revealed when the will was opened on 18 March, his adopted son and principal heir.

Caesar's funeral, held probably on 20 March in the Forum, was the turning point. Antony's oration, the historical form of the speech Shakespeare would give him, displayed the dictator's blood-stained toga, read out the terms of the will (which left seventy-five denarii to every Roman citizen and Caesar's private gardens across the Tiber to the public), and roused the crowd to a fury that swept the assassins out of Rome. Brutus and Cassius left Italy for their allotted provinces in the east, gathering armies and money.

Octavian, then only eighteen, arrived in Italy in early April 44 BCE from his studies at Apollonia in Illyria, formally accepted the adoption, took the name Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus, and began recruiting Caesar's veterans on his own account. A fluid and chaotic year followed, with Octavian, Antony and Cicero jockeying against one another, before the young Caesar, the veteran general Antony, and the Caesarian Marcus Aemilius Lepidus formalised the Second Triumvirate at Bononia in November 43 BCE, by a lex Titia that gave them dictatorial power for five years. Proscriptions followed at once: Cicero, whose Philippic speeches had denounced Antony, was hunted down and killed on 7 December 43 BCE.

In 42 BCE the triumviral armies crossed to Macedonia and defeated Brutus and Cassius in two engagements at Philippi in October and November. Cassius killed himself after the first battle, Brutus after the second. The Republic the Liberators had killed Caesar to preserve was, effectively, dead with them. A further thirteen years of civil war between the triumvirs themselves, above all between Octavian and Antony (who was now allied with Cleopatra VII of Egypt), ended at Actium in September 31 BCE. In January 27 BCE the Senate formally granted the young Caesar the name Augustus. Rome was, in all but name, a monarchy.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Ides of March became, and have remained, one of the classic dates of European political memory. Roman writers from Cicero's letters and Nicolaus of Damascus's Life of Augustus onwards, through Suetonius, Plutarch, Appian and Cassius Dio, produced an interlocking body of narrative that fixed the assassination as a moral drama with clearly named parts. That tradition passed through Petrarch and the Italian humanists into the political imagination of Renaissance Europe. Dante placed Brutus and Cassius in the lowest circle of Hell, chewed eternally in the mouths of Satan alongside Judas Iscariot. Shakespeare's Julius Caesar, written in 1599 and drawing chiefly on Sir Thomas North's 1579 English translation of Plutarch, has been for four centuries the most influential single depiction of the event; its Et tu, Brute? and Cry havoc, and let slip the dogs of war are among the best-known lines in the English language.

The assassination has been argued and re-argued in every political register since. To eighteenth-century French revolutionaries, Brutus was a hero of tyrannicide, his marble bust prominent in David's paintings of the Convention. To nineteenth-century monarchists, he was a naïve destroyer of the peace Caesar had brought after decades of civil war. Modern historians, from Ronald Syme in The Roman Revolution (1939) to more recent scholars such as Mary Beard, tend to see both Caesar and the Liberators as products of a longer breakdown of the Republican constitution, in which the concentration of army command, provincial wealth and personal patronage in the hands of individual senators had made a return to genuine Republican norms impossible.

The physical site is one of the best preserved of any political killing in history. Excavations at the Largo di Torre Argentina in central Rome in 1926 to 1929, extended in the twenty-first century by the Sovrintendenza Capitolina, exposed the whole ground plan of the Curia of Pompey, whose remains, marked by a bronze plaque set into the pavement at the ancient level, are now open to visitors as part of the Largo Argentina archaeological area. The Theatre of Pompey survives fragmentarily inside the buildings of the Campo de' Fiori quarter; the curve of its cavea is preserved in the curved street plan of the Via di Grotta Pinta. Every year on 15 March a small group of Romans still lays flowers at the spot.

worldContext.text: Ptolemaic Egypt, the last independent Hellenistic kingdom, was in the ninth year of the reign of Cleopatra VII. Her Alexandrian court had received Caesar in 48 to 47 BCE, and their son Caesarion had been born in 47 BCE; she had visited Rome in the autumn of 45 BCE and was still there when Caesar was killed. To the east, the Parthian Empire under Orodes II ruled from the Euphrates to the Iranian plateau; Caesar's planned expedition against Parthia was to have set out three days after his death. Judaea was still ruled by the Hasmonean high priest Hyrcanus II under Roman client status.

The Han dynasty in China was in its middle phase, under Emperor Yuan (r. 48 to 33 BCE), whose reign saw further consolidation of Confucian orthodoxy in state doctrine, the extension of the imperial university (the Taixue), and the acceptance of the Chinese calendar reforms of 104 BCE that had put the Han state on a firm astronomical footing. On the northern frontier the Xiongnu confederation had recently split into northern and southern factions, and the southern shanyu was formally submitting to Han overlordship.

In north-western India the Indo-Greek kingdoms established after Alexander were fragmenting under pressure from the Indo-Scythian (Saka) invaders sweeping down from Central Asia. The last Indo-Greek king, Strato II, would rule in the eastern Punjab into the early first century BCE. The Indo-Scythians were consolidating power at Taxila and Mathura. On the Gangetic plain, the Shunga and Kanva dynasties were in decline, and regional kingdoms in the Deccan and the far south were consolidating.

In Mesoamerica the great city of Teotihuacan in the Basin of Mexico was in its early formative phase; its first monumental pyramids, the Pyramid of the Sun and the Pyramid of the Moon, would be raised over the next century. In the Maya lowlands the Late Preclassic period was underway, with major building at Cerros, Cival and the Mirador basin. In the Andes the Nazca and Moche cultures were emerging on the Peruvian coast, the Nazca beginning to lay out the famous ground drawings.

In the middle Nile the Meroitic kingdom of Kush continued to build royal pyramids at Meroe and had, under the kandake (queen) Amanirenas, come into conflict with the Roman frontier in Egypt following the Roman annexation of Ptolemaic Egypt after Actium, still thirteen years in the future in 44 BCE. In North Africa the Numidian kingdom of Juba I had been absorbed into the Roman province of Africa Nova after his defeat at Thapsus in 46 BCE, and the Kingdom of Mauretania continued as a Roman client state further west.

In the western Pacific ancestral Polynesian societies were consolidating in Tonga, Samoa and Fiji, developing the chiefly hierarchies, canoe technology and horticultural repertoire that would in subsequent centuries carry Polynesians eastward to the Society and Marquesas Islands, and, a millennium later, to Hawaii and Aotearoa. In Australia, Aboriginal populations continued the world's oldest unbroken cultural tradition, sustaining complex kin systems, rock-art traditions and ecological management across the continent.

figures.name: Gaius Julius Caesar
figures.role: Roman general, dictator perpetuo from February 44 BCE, assassinated on the Ides of March at the age of 55

figures.name: Marcus Junius Brutus
figures.role: Praetor for 44 BCE, moral figurehead of the conspiracy, descended by tradition from the founder of the Republic

figures.name: Gaius Cassius Longinus
figures.role: Praetor and Brutus's brother-in-law, principal organiser of the plot

figures.name: Decimus Junius Brutus Albinus
figures.role: Senior Caesarian officer and one of Caesar's heirs, whose personal appeal persuaded Caesar to attend the fatal Senate meeting

figures.name: Mark Antony
figures.role: Caesar's co-consul, detained outside the Curia during the killing, whose funeral oration turned Roman opinion against the Liberators

figures.name: Gaius Octavius (Augustus)
figures.role: Caesar's eighteen-year-old great-nephew, named principal heir in the will opened three days after the assassination; later first Roman emperor

images.caption: La morte di Cesare, by Vincenzo Camuccini, 1804 to 1805. Galleria Nazionale d'Arte Moderna e Contemporanea, Rome. Caesar collapses at the foot of Pompey's statue as the Liberators lift their daggers.
images.credit: Vincenzo Camuccini (1771 to 1844), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Tusculum portrait of Julius Caesar, in Lunense marble, c. 50 to 40 BCE, the only surviving securely identified portrait of Caesar carved during his lifetime. Museum of Antiquities, Turin.
images.credit: Photograph by Ángel M. Felicísimo, via Wikimedia Commons
